segments.s707-g1: This is a prepositional/location phrase putting the subject in space: ازین سوی (از + این) = 'from this side' + خسرو (the subject). بران رزمگاه is a classical locative: بران (thereon/thence) + رزمگاه 'battlefield' — word order is somewhat inverted (prepositional phrase before the subject). Together they mean 'from this side Khosrow (toward) that battlefield.'
segments.s707-g2: بیامد is simple past 'came.' که here introduces the subordinate idea giving the reason or circumstance ('for/because' or 'when'); the remainder names the cause: بهرام بد با سپاه = 'Bahram (the bad/ill) with an army.' The clause reads as a causal/temporal complement: '...came, for Bahram had come with an army.'
segments.s707-g3: همه رزمگاهش is a quantified possessed noun: 'his whole battlefield' (همه + رزمگاه + -ش possessive). به تاراج داد is a verb phrase with به + noun as goal/purpose: 'gave to pillage' — داد is simple past 'gave,' so the whole means 'he gave the entire battlefield over to pillage.'
segments.s707-g4: سپه را uses را as the definite object marker ('the army'); همه quantifies the following nouns. بدره (poetic 'full-moon' = beautiful women/beauties) and تاج 'crown' are the direct objects of داد 'gave.' The clause says he 'gave the army all (i.e. beauties) and the crown.'
segments.s707-g5: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'at one time / suddenly / at once' (a certain instant). تیز رو is a compound adjective 'swift-moving' or 'swift-riding' and functions as the noun's descriptor. برنشست is past tense 'he mounted/sat up' (بر- prefix + نشست), so the clause describes a sudden mounting by a swift rider.
segments.s707-g6: میان را: میان 'the middle/center' with را marking it as the definite object. ز بهر (ز + بهر) = 'for the sake of / because of' and پرستش 'worship'/'homage.' ببست is archaic past 'he closed/fastened' (ب-بست). Together the clause says he 'shut/blocked the center for the purpose of homage/adoration,' i.e., he closed off the middle to allow or enforce acts of homage.
segments.s707-g7: به پیش اندر آمد is a classical/periphrastic way of saying 'came forward' (به پیش = forward/before; اندر آمد = entered/approached). یکی خارستان is 'a certain Khārastān' (یکی used as 'a certain' + خارستان, here functioning as a personal name or epithet). The phrase introduces this character coming forward.
segments.s707-g8: پیاده ببود uses the archaic copula ببود (past of بودن) with پیاده 'on foot/infantry' — 'he was on foot.' اندر/اندران کارستان: اندرران (here given as اندر‌ان) = 'therein/in him' and کارستان 'feat/marvel' — the idiom says 'in him was prowess' (i.e. he had notable feats/bravery). The structure is classical: copula + locative/inward phrase describing qualities.
segments.s707-g17: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' and it introduces a subordinate purpose/command clause with تا ('so that/in order that'). The clause تا پیش او شد دبیر places the verb شد (past of شدن) after the subordinating تا; پیش او = 'in his presence' (preposition + pronoun) and دبیر is the patient/appointed person 'scribe.' The word order (verb introducing the subordinate clause, then prepositional phrase, then past verb + noun) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s707-g18: نوشتند is a past plural 'they wrote' (an agentive past); ز + و here encode 'from him / by him' (poetic z- + the pronoun), marking the source/author of the letter. نامه‌ای is an indefinite object 'a letter' and برحریر is a prepositional adverbial 'on (of) silk' (برحریر = 'on silk'). The clause is a straightforward past passive/agentive construction: 'they wrote (a letter) from him on silk.'
segments.s707-g19: ز introduces the phrase as 'about / from' (poetic usage) and begins a relative clause. چیزی که = 'that which / something that' — a free relative; رفت is the simple past 'went / happened' and here functions as 'occurred' in the clause. اندران = 'in/therein' marks location, and رزمگاه is the locative noun 'battlefield.' Together the segment means 'about what occurred in that battlefield.'
segments.s707-g20: به قیصر = 'to Caesar' (به = to/for + proper name). نوشت is past 'wrote' and introduces the indirect object; اندران here repeats the locative/adverbial 'in that' (i.e., 'in that letter' or 'therein') and نامه is the direct object 'letter.' شاه ('the king') is the agent/author of the action. Word order places the indirect object (به قیصر) before the verb, with the thing written (نامه) following the locative.
segments.s707-g21: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first.' آفرین here is the noun 'praise/acclamation' used with the light verb کرد to form 'praised' (آفرین کرد = 'uttered praise/praised'). بر introduces the target of praise ('upon/for') and دادگر is the noun 'the just one/just ruler.' This is a common classical construction: adverb + object (آفرین) + light verb (کرد) + prepositional target (بر دادگر).
segments.s707-g22: کزو = 'from whom / in him' identifies the source/subject being described; دید is the past 'he saw / perceived' and takes the following nouns as its objects/qualities perceived. The sequence مردی و بخت و هنر lists the perceived attributes: 'manliness (or a man), fortune, and skill.' Classical Persian often puts the source pronoun (کزو) before the verb and then the things perceived after the verb.
segments.s707-g23: دگر here means 'again/once more' and modifies گفت (said). کز is the classical short form meaning 'from/that from' and introduces the source phrase; کردگار جهان = 'the Maker/Creator of the world' (کردگار = Creator, جهان = world). The whole segment is the preface to a statement of what he claimed to have seen from that source.
segments.s707-g24: همه نیکوی = 'all goodness / all good things' (همه = all, نیکوی = good/pleasant things). دیدم is first-person past 'I saw' (the speaker's perception), and اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret / in hiddenness' (اندر = in, نهان = hidden). The order places the object before the verb (common in classical Persian) and ends with the locative/adverbial phrase.
segments.s707-g73: پذیره is a noun here meaning 'a reception/party sent to receive someone' and functions as the object of فرستاد. فرستاد is the past tense 'sent'. خسرو is the subject (the proper name Khosrow) placed after the verb in this poetic word order. سوار is a noun 'horseman/rider' describing the sort of people sent.
segments.s707-g74: This phrase enumerates and qualifies the men sent: گرانمایگان is a plural noun 'illustrious/noble persons' and گرامی is an adjective 'honored/esteemed' modifying them; the omitted هزار (thousand) in the line gives the number.
segments.s707-g75: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones/nobles'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'towards/near to' (به + نزدِیک) and takes خسرو as its object. شدند is the past plural of شدن 'they came / became', here best rendered 'they came (near)'.
segments.s707-g76: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; پاک here describes their appearance or state ('clean/neat, properly dressed'); با هدیه نو is a prepositional phrase 'with new gifts' (با = 'with', هدیه = 'gift', نو = 'new'); شدند again is past 'they were/became' — i.e. they appeared thus.
segments.s707-g77: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the subject. نگه کرد is a compound of نگه (gaze/look) + کرد ('did/made'), meaning 'he looked'. و 'and' links the clauses. نامه بخواند is 'he read the letter' (بخواند here is narrative past 'read').
segments.s707-g78: ازان 'from that / at that' points back to the content just read; خواسته is the noun 'request/wish' referring to what was asked; در شگفتی is a locative phrase 'in astonishment'; بماند is the past of ماندن 'he remained/was left', so the whole means 'he remained in astonishment at that request.'
segments.s707-g79: به دستور is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/at the command (of)' (به used as 'by/with'). فرمود is the past tense verb 'he said/ordered' (here delivering a statement or command). پس 'then' marks sequence, and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s707-g80: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'. آن جامه روم is 'that garment of Rome/the Roman robe' (آن = that, جامه = garment/robe, روم = Byzantium/the Romans). گوهر نگار is a compound description meaning 'adorned with gems' (گوهر = gem; نگار here functions adjectivally as 'ornamented/embellished').
segments.s707-g41: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. آن نامه بر‌خواند is a perfective past (برخواند) meaning 'read aloud/read' with the object نامه; the subject قیصر (Caesar) and the prepositional phrase ز تخت ('from the throne') are placed after the verb in the line but grammatically attach to the following verbal phrase — classical/poetic word order allows these elements to be split across the line break.
segments.s707-g42: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'came down/descended' (فرود as the nominal/participial element + آمد as the past of آمدن). The clause shows verb then subject order (فرود آمد آن مرد), typical in epic/poetic Persian; بیداربخت is a postpositive adjective 'ill‑fated' modifying مرد, and آن is the demonstrative determiner.
segments.s707-g43: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to God' (یزدان = God). چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = thus; گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' in narrative). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly, and رهنمای is the vocative noun 'guide.'
segments.s707-g44: همیشه is an adverb 'always/forever.' توی + جاودانه form a locative phrase 'in the eternal (place/abode)' (توی = 'in/among', جاودانه = 'eternal'). بجای here functions as 'in the place / remain in the place' (used elliptically to mean 'may you be/remain in that place'); the whole phrase expresses a wish for perpetual abiding in the eternal abode.
segments.s707-g45: تو پیروز کردی is 'you made (someone) victorious' (پیروز here adjectival/nominal meaning 'victorious', کردی is the past of کردن used causatively/'made'). مر is the dative/benefactive pronoun 'to/for me', placed before the direct object; آن بنده را is the definite direct object ('that servant') marked by را. The verb کردی governs this object phrase: 'you made that servant (victorious) for me.'
segments.s707-g46: This noun phrase is a second object governed by the same verb کردی (i.e., a coordinated result): کشنده is a noun/adjective 'the murderous/killer (one)'; توی introduces a locative/relational phrase 'among/in' linked to مرد افگنده — مرد افگنده is 'the man who has been cast/thrown down' (افگنده as past participle/adjective). The final را marks the whole phrase as an object; syntactically the earlier verb 'کردی' applies to it (you laid low/the killer was made to be a fallen man).
segments.s707-g47: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly/much' placed frontally for emphasis. به درویش is the indirect object 'to the dervish'; دینار is the (definite/indefinite) direct object 'dinar(s)'; داد is the past tense verb 'gave.' Word order here is adverb + indirect object + direct object + verb, common in Persian narrative.
segments.s707-g48: همان refers back to the previous act ('the same' / 'likewise'), i.e., similarly/again. خوردنیهای بسیار is a noun phrase 'a great many edibles/foods' (خوردنیهای = edibles/food, بسیار = many/much) and داد repeats the verb 'gave.' The clause restates that, in addition to money, he gave abundant food.
segments.s707-g57: This clause uses the negative imperative construction: مکن (do not) governs the whole clause. جز از ... means 'except for ...' (جز + از functioning together as a restriction). داد و خوبی is a coordinated noun phrase ('justice and goodness'); در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s707-g58: The paired particles چه ... و چه ... form a correlative 'whether ... or ...'. در آشکار and اندر نهان are adverbial/locative phrases: در آشکار = 'in the open / openly', اندر نهان = archaic phrasing for 'in secret / secretly'. The conjunction و links the two alternatives.
segments.s707-g59: یکی تاج is a noun phrase 'a certain/one crown'. کز marks a relative/origin clause ('which/from which'), so کز قیصران یادگار reads 'a memento from the Caesars' (کز here indicates source). The whole segment is a nominal phrase describing the object possessed.
segments.s707-g60: همی‌داشتی is an archaic/imperfect second-person-singular form meaning 'you used to have' or 'you kept'. تا کی introduces a temporal/interrogative sense 'until when / how long', with کی functioning as 'when'. آید به کار literally 'comes to work' = 'becomes of use'; the verb آید is present/future in form but here part of the rhetorical question 'how long will it be useful?'.
segments.s707-g61: This is a nominal list introduced by همان 'that/the same' with خسروی as an epithet/substantive 'the kingly one' or 'sovereign'. طوق با گوشوار is a noun phrase 'a necklace/collar with earrings'; the verb ('had' or 'there was') is omitted and understood from the catalogue style of the passage.
segments.s707-g62: صدوشست is a compound numeral 'one hundred and sixty'. تا here functions as an extent marker 'even/up to' linking the numeral to the following noun phrase; جامه زرنگار is 'garment(s) of brocade/gilding' (زرنگار = brocade/gilded). Grammatically the numeral quantifies جامه زرنگار.
segments.s707-g63: دگر 'another/in addition' modifies the following quantified noun phrase. The numeral سی precedes شتر as usual. شتر بار دینار is 'camels loaded with dinars' (بار = load/cargo, دینار = dinars), and بود is the past-tense copula 'there was/were'. Word order is numeral → noun → modifier → verb.
segments.s707-g64: همان resumes 'the same' / 'likewise' referring back to other valuable items. در و یاقوت is a coordinated noun phrase '... and rubies' (here در is the poetic word for pearls, paired with یاقوت 'rubies'). بسیار بود is a predicative phrase 'there was much / there were many' (بسیار = abundant/many; بود = was/were).
segments.s707-g25: به + proper name + آمدم is a typical Persian prepositional phrase + past verb: 'به آذرگشسپ آمدم' = 'I came to Āzar-Gošasp.' آمدم is 1st-person past 'I came.' با سپاه follows as an instrumental/acomitative phrase 'with an army.' Word order is prepositional phrase + verb + accompaniment.
segments.s707-g26: دوان is an adverbial/manner word 'running/rushing' modifying the verb بازآمدم 'I returned/came back' (past 1st). پیش here means 'forward/before' (motion toward/forward), and کینه خواه is a descriptive compound: کینه 'hatred/grudge' + خواه 'desiring' => 'seeking vengeance' (present participial/ adjective sense). Together: 'I rushed forward and came back, seeking vengeance.'
segments.s707-g27: بدان گونه is a manner phrase 'in that way/in that manner' (بدان = 'in that', گونه = 'manner, way'), تنگ is an adjectival/adverbial 'close/tight,' and اندر آمد به جنگ is the verb phrase 'entered into battle' (اندر آمد = 'came/entered in', به جنگ = 'into the fight'). Word order: manner phrase precedes the verb phrase.
segments.s707-g28: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/whereby.' بر من = 'against me/to me,' ببد is the archaic past 'was' (here 'became'), کار پیکار = 'the affair of battle' (i.e. the fight), and تنگ 'narrow/tight' idiomatically means 'difficult/adverse.' The clause means 'so that the fight became adverse for me.'
segments.s707-g29: چو here = 'when/if.' یزدان پاکش = 'his pure God' (یزدان 'God' + پاکش 'pure' with possessive feel), نبد is the negative/archaic 'was not' (did not exist), and دستگیر here is the benevolent meaning 'one who gives a helping hand/aid.' Together: 'When his pure God was not his helper/aid...' (conditional/temporal clause).
segments.s707-g30: بمرد is an optative/curse-form 'may he die' (expressing a wish or doom). آن دم = 'at that moment/then,' followed by a triad of punishments listed by nouns joined with و: آتش 'fire,' دار here 'gallows' (a punitive instrument), and گیر 'seizure/capture' (the noun 'catch'/'seizure'). The phrase curses death by these means.
segments.s707-g31: چوبیچاره‌تر گشت is a predicative phrase 'became more pitiable/more helpless' (گشت = 'became'). و لشکر نماند = 'and no army remained' (نماند is past negative 'did not remain / was not left'). Subject is understood from context; two coordinated clauses describe the result.
segments.s707-g32: گریزان is a participial adjective 'the fleeing/flighty ones.' به شبگیر is an adverbial time phrase 'at night/at nightfall' (شبگیر 'nocturnal/night-time'), ز آنجا 'from there' (ز = 'from'), and براند is a past-tense (archaic) verb 'drove away/expelled.' The sequence says that the fugitives he drove away from that place at night.
segments.s707-g9: به + غلتید is a directional prepositional phrase: به (towards/into) + غلتید (past tense, 3rd person singular ‘he rolled/fell’). در پیش یزدان is a locative phrase meaning ‘in the presence of God’ (در = in/at, پیش = before/in the presence of). The final به خاک repeats the directional به and names the goal (خاک = earth/dust), so the whole segment says he rolled down onto the earth before God.
segments.s707-g10: همی‌گفت is an archaic progressive/perfective form: همی (iterative marker) + گفت (past ‘said’) giving ‘kept saying’ or ‘was saying repeatedly.’ کای is the vocative particle “O,” introducing direct address. داور is the addressee (‘Judge’), and داد و پاک are two descriptive nouns/adjectives in parataxis (‘justice’ and ‘pure’), functioning as epithets of the Judge.
segments.s707-g11: پی here means ‘after’ / ‘in pursuit of’ (followed by the object); دشمن is the direct object ‘the enemy.’ از بوم is a prepositional phrase ‘from the homeland’ (از = from, بوم = homeland). برداشتی is 2nd-person past (‘you took/removed’) used here idiomatically to indicate leaving or withdrawing from the homeland.
segments.s707-g12: همه کار is ‘all affairs’ (همه = all, کار = work/affairs). ز is the poetic form of از meaning ‘from’ or ‘out of,’ and اندیشه is ‘thought/counsel.’ بگذاشتی is past 2nd-person with the prefix ب- (here meaning ‘you put aside’ / ‘you abandoned’), so the phrase states you abandoned all affairs and counsel.
segments.s707-g13: پرستنده is a noun ‘worshipper/devotee.’ و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ ناسزا is a noun meaning ‘insult/abuse’ used here adjectivally or adverbially with بنده‌ام to convey that the speaker is a servant who is subjected to reproach or is blameworthy. بنده‌ام is the first-person copular form ‘I am a servant’ (بنده + ـام).
segments.s707-g14: به introduces the prepositional phrase ‘before/at’ or ‘under’ (here ‘before/under the authority of’). فرمان is ‘command’ and رایت is ‘banner/standard’; the two are joined by و. سرافگنده‌ام is a stative first-person form ‘I am ashamed / I am disgraced’ (سـر + افگنده + ـام), so the clause says the speaker feels shame in relation to the command and banner.
segments.s707-g15: وزان is a compound of و + ز آن meaning ‘and from that / from that place/time.’ جایگه is ‘place/position,’ and شد is the past of شدن ‘became/went/happened.’ به پرده سرای is a directional phrase introduced by به (‘to/towards’) with پرده ‘curtain’ and سرای indicating a hall/chamber; together they mean ‘to the curtained chamber.’
segments.s707-g16: بیامد is past tense ‘he came.’ به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase ‘to the near/nearby’ (i.e. ‘near to’), او is the third-person pronoun ‘him,’ and رهنمای (here postposed, a common poetic word order) is the noun ‘guide,’ so the clause reads ‘a guide came near him’ with the verb placed before its nominal subject.
segments.s707-g33: همه لشکرش را is the definite object phrase: همه modifies لشکرش (“his whole army”), and را is the Persian definite/object marker placed after the noun. بهم بر زدیم is a compound verbal phrase: زدیم is the 1st-person plural past of زدن (“to strike/attack”), with the prefix/element بر (here as part of the phrasal verb برزدن) and the adverbial بهم giving the sense of scattering/disordering — together yielding the idiomatic meaning “we routed/scattered (them).”
segments.s707-g34: به لشکر marks the target/direction (“against/into the army”). گهش is an archaic/poetic form (گه + -ش) here functioning roughly as “in it / among them” (an older locative/possessive/occurrent element); آتش is the noun object “fire”; اندرزدیم is a past 1st‑person‑plural verb formed from اندر زدن meaning “to put/throw into” or “to set in” — used here for “we caused fire among them / we set (parts of) the host on fire.”
segments.s707-g35: به فرمان introduces an instrumental/agent phrase “by the command (of ...)”; یزدان is the divine noun “God” and پیروزگر is an adjective/epithet following it, “victorious/triumphant,” so the whole phrase means “by the command of the victorious God.”
segments.s707-g36: ببندم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive form of بستن used in narrative to express the action “I close / I have closed.” The following words form the complement/goal: برو (here an independent verb-form meaning “go” or a classical verbal particle), نیز “also,” and راه گذر “the road/passage/pass” (راه = way, گذر = pass/passage). Together the clause expresses that, by that command, the speaker closed or blocked the passage (and/or likewise took action regarding the road).
segments.s707-g37: نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن “to place/present,” so نهادند برنامه بر مهرشاه means “they presented/placed the plan before/to Mehrshah.” Word order places برنامه before the verb for emphasis; بر introduces the indirect object/recipient (مهرشاه).
segments.s707-g38: فرستادگان is the subject “the envoys.” گرفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past of گرفتن “to take/seize”; here بر is a directional/serial particle and راه is the object “road/pass.” The phrase بر گرفتند راه (word order inverted) means “they took the road” — i.e., they set out on the route.
segments.s707-g39: فرستاده is the noun “messenger/envoy.” با + نامه indicates accompaniment or instrument “with a letter,” and شهریار names the sender/owner of the letter (“the king”); together: “the messenger with the king’s letter” (i.e., carrying the shah’s letter).
segments.s707-g40: بشد is a narrative/past form meaning roughly “he went / it happened that he went” (from شدن used here intransitively to mark movement or occurrence). تا here means “until/as far as” introducing the goal, and بر قیصر نامدار means “to the famed Caesar” (بر = toward/to, قیصر = Caesar/emperor, نامدار = renowned). Together: “he went/reached to the renowned Caesar.”
segments.s707-g65: صلیبی is used as a noun meaning 'a Crusader/Christian' (not the literal adjective 'cruciform'); فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' with the subject before the verb (subject–verb order). گوهر نگار forms a compound noun phrase meaning 'adorned/ornamented with gems' (گوهر 'gem' + نگار used here adjectivally/compounding to mean 'gem‑adorned').
segments.s707-g66: یکی introduces a single item ('one' / 'a certain'); تخت is the head noun 'throne'; پرگوهر is a prefixed compound meaning 'full of gems' or 'jeweled' (پر- = 'full of' + گوهر); شاهوار is a postpositive attributive epithet 'regal'/'kingly' describing the throne.
segments.s707-g67: یکی introduces the item ('one/a certain'); سبز is the color adjective 'green'; خفتان here names a garment/coverlet (an archaic/poetic noun — not the literal verb 'to sleep'); به marks the material/instrument ('with/by' in poetic usage) and زَر is the material 'gold'; بافته is a past participle 'woven' modifying the garment.
segments.s707-g68: بسی here means 'many/much' (poetic intensifier) though its listed senses do not match exactly; شوشه زر = 'golden tassels/plumes' (شوشه 'tassel', زَر 'gold'). برو is a compact/poetic locative/relational element meaning roughly 'on it' / 'upon' (not the literal verb 'go'), and تافته is a participial adjective 'woven/embroidered' describing how the tassels are set on the cloth.
segments.s707-g69: ازان is a compound/prepositional pointer 'of/from those' referring back to previously mentioned people; فیلسوفان is the plural 'philosophers' (subject) and رومی is the qualifier 'Roman/Byzantine' specifying their origin.
segments.s707-g70: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/they departed'; با marks accompaniment 'with' and هدیه 'gifts' names what they carried. The token shown as وبا in the segmentation is actually 'و با' ('and with') linking the next noun; نثار is 'offerings/dedications' — together the phrase means they went bearing gifts and offerings.
segments.s707-g71: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; زان refers back to 'those' ('from that' / 'of those'); کارها is the plural 'deeds/matters'; شد is the past intransitive 'came to pass'/'happened'; به شاه آگهی is the prepositional phrase 'information reached the king' (به marks the target and آگهی = 'news/information').
segments.s707-g72: ز is the source/preposition 'from'; قیصر names 'the Caesar/emperor' as source or interlocutor; شدش is شد + enclitic ش ('it became his' / 'it fell to him') indicating result to/for him; کاربا is the fused phrase کار + با ('dealings/affairs with') used here as a unit meaning 'dealings with'; فرهی is an adjectival epithet 'noble/august' qualifying those dealings (i.e., they became honorable).
segments.s707-g81: نه negates the clause. آیین پرمایه دهقان is a noun phrase (آیین 'custom/rite' modified by adjective پرمایه 'substantial/wealthy', followed by دهقان 'dehqān' meaning the landed gentry); بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is Subject–Predicate with the verb final, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s707-g82: کجا introduces a locus clause 'where' (rhetorical: 'a place where...'). جامه جاثلیقان is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'the robe of the jāṯaliq/Catholicos' (classical Persian often omits the linking ezāfe in verse). بود is again the past copula 'was'.
segments.s707-g83: چو = 'when'. بر جامه ما = prepositional phrase 'on our garment' (بر + noun + possessive pronoun ما after the noun, as is normal). چلیپا بود = 'there was a cross' (چلیپا 'cross' + بود past copula); the clause states a temporal condition.
segments.s707-g84: نشست is used as a past action 'sat' (verb), with an implicit or previously established subject; اندر آیین ترسا = 'in the ritual/assembly of the pious' (اندر = 'in/within', آیین = 'ritual/ceremony', ترسا = 'pious person'). بود again appears as past copula, reinforcing past tense.
segments.s707-g85: وگر = 'if' (introducing a conditional). خود is emphatic 'myself'. نپوشم is 1st person singular negative present/subjunctive 'I do not wear' (object 'it'—the garment—is implied). بیازارد is a third-person form 'will cause pain/annoy' or 'be offended'; the following اوی is the third-person pronoun (subject/affected person) placed after the verb, a common poetic word order V–S.
segments.s707-g86: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily'/'indeed'. دگرگونه is an adverb/adjunct 'otherwise' or 'in a different way'. پندارد here means 'he will judge/think' (verb sense: to hold an opinion), and اوی again is the third-person pronoun 'he' at the clause end — the sentence says he will form a different opinion.
segments.s707-g87: وگر = 'if' (conditional). پوشم is 1st person present/subjunctive 'I wear' (or 'I put on'). این نامداران همه = 'all these renowned men' (این 'these', نامداران 'renowned ones', همه 'all' placed after the noun for emphasis). The clause sets a hypothetical result.
segments.s707-g88: بگویند is a third-person plural verb form 'they will say' (the provided lexical senses list gives noun senses for the root, so the precise verbal sense is not listed there). کاین = 'that this/that' introducing reported speech. شهریار = 'sovereign/king'. رمه = 'flock/herd' used here metaphorically; the phrase means 'they will call this king a herd' (i.e. treat him as common or as one of the crowd).
segments.s707-g49: مر is an enclitic/old-fashioned object pronoun meaning 'to me' placed before the object for emphasis. آن نامه را is the definite direct object ('that letter' + object marker را). نیز 'also' links this action to the previous gifts. پاسخ نوشت is a simple past predicate 'wrote a reply' (نوشت = he wrote); the subject (he) is omitted as usual.
segments.s707-g50: بسان introduces a simile 'like/as'. درختی is indefinite 'a tree' (the -ی ending makes it an indefinite or descriptive noun). به here functions as a locative/towardive preposition ('in/into/toward the garden') and باغ بهشت 'the garden of Paradise' is the locative phrase that completes the simile.
segments.s707-g51: سرنامه 'heading/prefatory heading' + کرد (made/did) is a light-verb construction meaning 'he made/wrote as the head of the letter'. از here means 'of/regarding' and جهاندار 'the sovereign' names whose mention follows; یاد 'remembrance/mention' is the noun object of سرنامه کرد ('he made/placed the remembrance of the sovereign [as the heading]').
segments.s707-g52: This short clause reads like the content of that heading or a blessing. خداوند here functions as the divine/vocative subject ('May God…'). پیروزی 'victory' is the first object; و simply links items. فرو with داد literally 'gave down' (an idiomatic poetic phrasing meaning 'bestowed' or 'sent down'), and داد is a past-tense verb 'gave/bestowed' (subject understood as خداوند in the blessing formula).
segments.s707-g53: خداوند + noun is a standard formula meaning 'the Lord/master of X' or an honorific title; here خداوند ماه = 'Lord of the Moon' and خداوند هور = 'Lord of Light/Splendor'. و connects the two parallel epithets. Word order is noun (title) followed by its domain.
segments.s707-g54: خداونت is an archaic/poetic form with the second-person possessive suffix -ت ('your lord' or 'your lordship') used contrastively here; پیل 'elephant' and مور 'ant' are paired as a contrast of great and small. The clause lists contrasts (huge vs tiny) using repeated خداوند forms; the copula 'is' is omitted as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s707-g55: The clause places the objects before the verb in typical Persian order: بزرگی 'greatness' and نیک اختری 'auspicious/starry (fortune)' are the things to be recognized. ز (poetic for از) = 'from/of' links to the person referred to (him). شناس is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'know/be aware of' or 'be acquainted with' — here an instruction/imperative-like function: 'know/recognize (them)'.
segments.s707-g56: This closing clause contains a purpose/wish introduced by تا 'so that/in order that'. زنده باشی is a subjunctive/wish ('that you may be alive' or 'so long as you live'); باشی is subjunctive/optative here. سپاس is a closing word 'thanks' or 'praise'. The first two words (وزو دار) are elliptical/poetic here: they function to mean 'and hold/keep him' or 'keep him with you' (a compact phrasing), so the whole clause reads as a charge/blessing: 'and keep him so that you may live — thanks.'
segments.s707-g89: 'مگر' here introduces an exception/concessive idea ('unless'/'except that'). 'کز' (= که + ز) introduces the following clause ('that' / 'because'), 'پی' = 'after' or 'following', so 'کز پی چیز ترسا شدست' literally: 'that, after some thing, he became a ترسا (devout/pious person)'. 'شدست' is an archaic/poetic past perfective ('has become' / 'became'). Word order places the time/cause phrase before the predication.
segments.s707-g90: 'که' links this clause to the previous explanatory idea ('for/that'); 'اندر میان' is the locative phrase 'in the middle/among'; 'چلیپا' = 'cross'; 'شدست' again is the poetic past ('was made/appeared'). The clause literally says 'for a cross was made in the middle', with the verb at the end as usual in classical Persian.
segments.s707-g91: 'به خسرو' = 'to Khosrow' (recipient). 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus/so'; 'پس رهنمای' places 'then/the adviser' (رهنمای = the adviser) with 'پس' = 'then'. Note that 'گفت' is the reporting verb 'said' introducing the quoted content that follows.
segments.s707-g92: This is the content of the adviser’s speech. 'که' introduces the clause (here comparable to 'that' / introducing direct statement). 'دین نیست' = 'religion is not' (negative copula). 'شاها' is a vocative 'O king!'. 'به پوشش بپای' = 'does not abide in/consist in outward covering' — 'به' = 'in/by', 'پوشش' = 'covering/garment', 'بپای' is an intransitive/impersonal verb meaning 'to abide/consist' (here in negative: 'does not consist in clothing'). Word order places the vocative inside the clause; the existential/ontological sense 'دین نیست ...' is typical classical phrasing.
segments.s707-g93: 'تو' = 'you' (subject), 'بردین' is a past form 'you took' (archaic/poetic second-person past), followed by the object phrase 'زر دشت پیغمبری' — a genitive chain: 'the gold of the plain of prophethood' (i.e., 'the prophetic plain's gold' or 'the gold from the prophet's plain'). The noun modifying chain places the head noun 'زر' before its modifiers.
segments.s707-g94: 'اگر چند' is a concessive formula here ('although/even though'); classical Persian often uses 'اگر چند' to introduce a concession. 'پیوسته قیصری' lacks an explicit verb and reads as 'the Caesarean/imperial [rule] being continuous' — 'پیوسته' = 'continuous/ongoing' modifies 'قیصری' = 'imperial/Caesarean'. The meaning is 'even though the imperial authority remained continuous.'
segments.s707-g95: 'بپوشید' is a past/plural (poetic) verb 'they put on' / 'they clothed (him)'; subject is implied. 'پس' = 'then'. 'جامه شهریار' = 'the king's garment' (جامه modified by the genitive شهریار). The verb before its objects is common in the epic/poetic register.
segments.s707-g96: 'بیاویخت' is an archaic/past form 'they hung' or 'they fastened (on)'. 'آن تاج گوهرنگار' = 'that jewel-encrusted crown' with 'آن' = 'that/the', 'تاج' = 'crown' (head noun), and 'گوهرنگار' a compound adjective 'adorned with gems'. The verb precedes the noun phrase, and the adjective follows the noun as is typical.
segments.s707-g97: برفتند is a past 3rd-person plural verb 'they departed/left' placed before its subjects (typical V‑initial poetic word order). رومی و ایرانیان are the coordinated subjects ('the Roman(s) and the Iranians'), with رومی used as a collective/ethnic label and ایرانیان explicitly plural.
segments.s707-g98: ز هر گونه مردم اندر میان is a prepositional/participle phrase 'from/among every kind of people'; ز (poetic form of از) = 'from', هر = 'every', گونه = 'kind/type', مردم = 'people', and اندر میان together mean 'in/among' (a fossilized locative phrase). The whole phrase specifies the domain from which they departed.
segments.s707-g99: کسی کش خرد بود functions as an indefinite noun phrase + copula: کسی 'anyone/someone', کش is an agentive/intensive element forming a nominal description ('a person of ...'), خرد = 'wise/understanding' used adjectivally, and بود is the past copula 'was'. چون introduces a temporal clause 'when/as', جامه = 'robe/garment', دید = past 'saw'.
segments.s707-g100: بدانست is the past verb 'he/they knew it' (here 'knew/realized'); کو is an archaic interrogative/relative 'who/whom' (not covered in the provided sense options, so marked -1). رای قیصر گزید is a short clause 'had chosen the Caesar's opinion/choice' — رای here means 'decision/choice' and گزید is 'chose/selected', so the phrase means 'knew whom the Caesar had chosen'.
segments.s707-g101: دگر here means 'another' (someone else). گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided senses listed nouns like 'conversation' rather than the verb form, so its applicable verbal sense is not in the options and is marked -1). کاین شهریار جهان is the direct object/quoted content introducer: کاین = 'this', شهریار = 'king/ruler', جهان = 'of the world'.
segments.s707-g102: همانا is a particle meaning 'verily/indeed' emphasizing the statement. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. ترسا = 'a pious/devout person', شد = past of شدن 'became', and اندر نهان = 'in secret' (اندر = 'in/within', نهان used adverbially 'secretly'). The clause says he had become devout privately.

sentences.s707-s1: From this side Khosrow came to that battlefield, for Bahram the ill had come with an army.
sentences.s707-s2: He gave his entire battlefield up to pillage; he gave the army everything — moon-like beauties and the crown.
sentences.s707-s3: At once a swift-riding man mounted, and he closed the middle for the sake of homage.
sentences.s707-s4: Forward came a certain Khārastān; he was on foot, and within him was prowess.
sentences.s707-s9: He commanded that a scribe come before him; a letter from him was written on silk.
sentences.s707-s10: About what happened on that battlefield, the king wrote that very matter to Caesar in the letter.
sentences.s707-s11: First he praised the just man; of him he perceived manliness, good fortune, and skill.
sentences.s707-s12: Again he said that from the Maker of the world he had seen all goodness hidden.
sentences.s707-s37: Khosrow sent a reception — a thousand honored horsemen.
sentences.s707-s38: The nobles came near to Khosrow; all, cleanly, appeared with new gifts.
sentences.s707-s39: When Khosrow looked and read the letter, he remained astonished at that request.
sentences.s707-s40: By that command the king then said that that Roman garment was studded with jewels.
sentences.s707-s21: When Caesar read that letter, the ill-fated man descended from the throne.
sentences.s707-s22: He thus said to God: 'O guide, may you remain forever in the eternal abode.'
sentences.s707-s23: You granted victory to that servant of mine and laid low the murderous man.
sentences.s707-s24: He gave the dervish abundant dinars; likewise he gave a great deal of food.
sentences.s707-s29: Do nothing in the world except justice and goodness, whether openly or in secret.
sentences.s707-s30: You had a certain crown, a memento from the Caesars; how long would it be of use?
sentences.s707-s31: That same royal (one) — a collar with earrings, one hundred and sixty (even) brocaded garments.
sentences.s707-s32: In addition, thirty camels laden with dinars there were; likewise pearls and rubies were abundant.
sentences.s707-s13: I came to Āzar-Gošasp with an army; I ran forward and returned bent on revenge.
sentences.s707-s14: He entered the fight in that close manner, so that the battle turned out adverse for me.
sentences.s707-s15: When his pure God did not become a helper, then may he die at that moment by fire, by the gallows, and by capture.
sentences.s707-s16: He became even more pitiable and no army remained; the fleeing men he drove from that place by night.
sentences.s707-s5: He rolled onto the ground before God, repeatedly saying, “O Judge, giver of justice and pure one.”
sentences.s707-s6: Following the enemy you left your homeland; you abandoned every affair and counsel.
sentences.s707-s7: I am a worshipper and (though) reviled I am your servant; before your command and standard I am ashamed.
sentences.s707-s8: From that place he went to the curtained chamber; a guide came up near him.
sentences.s707-s17: We routed his whole army, and we set fire among his host.
sentences.s707-s18: By the command of the victorious God, I closed (the) pass as well.
sentences.s707-s19: They presented the plan to Mehrshah; the envoys then took the road.
sentences.s707-s20: The messenger, bearing the king's letter, set out to the famed Caesar.
sentences.s707-s33: A Crusader sent a jewel‑studded cross and a regal throne full of gems.
sentences.s707-s34: One green garment woven with gold; many gold tassels were woven upon it.
sentences.s707-s35: Four of those Roman (Byzantine) philosophers set out, bearing gifts and offerings.
sentences.s707-s36: When the king was informed of those deeds, his dealings with the Caesar became honorable.
sentences.s707-s41: It was not the wealthy custom of the dehqāns, nor a place where the Catholicos' robe belonged.
sentences.s707-s42: When there was a cross on our garment, he sat among the pious in their ceremony.
sentences.s707-s43: But if I myself do not wear it, he will be offended by it; indeed he will think differently of me.
sentences.s707-s44: And if I wear it, all these renowned men will say: 'This sovereign is a herd.'
sentences.s707-s25: He also wrote me an answer to that letter, like a tree in the garden of Paradise.
sentences.s707-s26: He put at the head of the letter a mention of the sovereign: 'May God grant victory and bestow (blessings).'
sentences.s707-s27: Lord of the Moon and Lord of Splendor; your lord (is) an elephant and the Lord (is) an ant.
sentences.s707-s28: Know his greatness and auspicious stellar sign; keep him so that you may live — thanks.
sentences.s707-s45: Unless, after some thing he became devout, for a cross had appeared in the middle.
sentences.s707-s46: Then the adviser said thus to Khosrow: 'O king, religion does not dwell in outward dress.'
sentences.s707-s47: You took the gold of the prophetic plain, even though the imperial (power) continued.
sentences.s707-s48: They clothed him in the king's robes, then hung that jewel-studded crown.
sentences.s707-s49: The Romans and the Iranians departed from among people of every kind.
sentences.s707-s50: Any man of understanding, when he saw the robe, knew whom the Caesar had chosen.
sentences.s707-s51: Another said that this king of the world had indeed become devout in secret.
